documentation.value: Prints its argument to the console. Dispatches to the appropriate method based on the class of x.
An object to be printed.
Additional arguments passed to methods.

signatures.label: cat(..., file = "", sep = " ", fill = FALSE, labels = NULL, append = FALSE)

documentation.value: Concatenates and prints objects. Unlike print(), does not add quotes around strings and does not print NULL.
The objects to concatenate and print.
A connection or file name to write to. "" means console output.
String to separate each object. Default is a single space.
Concatenates vectors after converting to character, separated by sep.
One or more objects to be converted to character vectors and concatenated.
String to separate the terms. Default is a single space.
Optional string to separate results. If set, collapses the result into a single string.
Concatenates vectors without any separator. Equivalent to paste(..., sep = "").
One or more objects to concatenate.
Optional string to collapse results into a single string.
Returns the length of a vector or list.
An R object. For vectors, returns the number of elements.

signatures.label: nchar(x, type = "bytes", allowNA = FALSE, keepNA = NA) -> integer

documentation.value: Returns the number of characters in a character vector.
A character vector.
Type of count: 'bytes', 'chars', or 'width'.

signatures.label: grep(pattern, x, ignore.case = FALSE, perl = FALSE, value = FALSE, fixed = FALSE) -> integer | character

documentation.value: Searches for matches to a pattern within elements of a character vector. Returns indices or matching values.
The regular expression pattern to match.
The character vector to search.
If TRUE, ignores case in matching.
If TRUE, returns matching elements instead of their indices.
If TRUE, pattern is a fixed string, not a regex.

signatures.label: gsub(pattern, replacement, x, ignore.case = FALSE, perl = FALSE, fixed = FALSE) -> character

documentation.value: Replaces all occurrences of a pattern in a character vector.
The regex pattern to match.
The replacement string. Use \\1, \\2 for backreferences.
The character vector.
If TRUE, treat pattern as a literal string.

signatures.label: sub(pattern, replacement, x, ignore.case = FALSE, perl = FALSE, fixed = FALSE) -> character

documentation.value: Replaces the first occurrence of a pattern in each element of a character vector.
The regex pattern to match.
The replacement string.
The character vector.
Combines its arguments to form a vector. All arguments are coerced to a common type.
Objects to combine. Can be atomic vectors, lists, or NULL.
Creates a list. Unlike c(), list elements can be of different types and remain as-is without coercion.
Objects to include. Can use name=value syntax for named elements.

signatures.label: data.frame(..., row.names = NULL, check.rows = FALSE, check.names = TRUE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) -> data.frame

documentation.value: Creates a data frame from the supplied vectors or lists. Columns must have the same number of rows.
Column definitions as name=vector pairs (e.g., x = 1:5, y = c('a','b','c','d','e')).
Row names for the data frame.
Whether to convert strings to factors. Default is FALSE since R 4.0.

signatures.label: read.csv(file, header = TRUE, sep = ",", quote = "\"", dec = ".", fill = TRUE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) -> data.frame

documentation.value: Reads a CSV file into a data frame.
The file path or URL to read from.
Whether the first row contains column names.
The field separator character. Default is comma.
Whether to convert strings to factors.
Writes a data frame to a CSV file.
The data frame to write.
The output file path.
Whether to include row names in the output.
Initializes a ggplot object. Used as the foundation for building layered graphics with the + operator.
A data frame to use for the plot. Can be overridden in individual layers.
Default aesthetic mappings created by aes().

signatures.label: aes(x, y, colour, fill, size, shape, alpha, group, ...) -> mapping

documentation.value: Constructs aesthetic mappings that describe how variables in the data are mapped to visual properties.
The variable mapped to the x-axis.
The variable mapped to the y-axis.
The variable mapped to colour/color.
The variable mapped to fill color.
